content.full_text:
STATE OF CALI FORNI A 
FAIR POLIT ICAL PRACT ICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite :iOOO • Sacramento, CA 9581 t 
( 9 I 6) :i 2 2-566 0 • Fa X ( 9 I 6) :12 2 -o 8 8 6 
Amy Webber 
City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
333 W. Ocean Boulevard, 11th Floor 
Long Beach, CA 90802-4664 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-206 
Dear Ms. Webber: 
September 13, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Section 82015 of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act")1 and ''behested payment" reporting. 
Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does the exception to the behested payment reporting requirement apply when the 
City's elected Mayor supports City staffs applications for grant funding by a written 
statement? 
2. Does the determination of whether the exception applies depend on the fonn of the 
written statement (i.e., separate letter, letter with photo, Signed versus unsigned 
statement)? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Payments solicited by the mayor for a governmental purpose must be reported if 
payments from a single source equal or exceed $5,000. There is no exception for payments solicited 
for the benefit of government employers. The exception in Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(ii) does not 
apply to payments solicited from a nonprofit for a governmental entity, but only to payments made 
fu!. a government entity at the behest of the official. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-206 
Page No. 2 
2. No. The exception would not apply under any of the facts you describe. 
FACTS 
The City of Long Beach utilizes a Council-Manager form of government (Long Beach City 
Charter section 102), in which "all powers of the City" are vested in the nine elected City 
Council members, and the nppointed City Manager administers the day-to-day operations of 
the City. 
The Mayor cannot vote, but presides over and participates in deliberations of the City 
Council, and" ... represents the City at large to provide community leadership." (Long Beach City 
Charter section 202{a).) In this role, the Mayor is called upon frequently to support grant 
applications from both public/governmental and private non-profit sources, all for legislative, 
governmental or charitable purposes. Applications will often suggest {or even require) expressions 
of support from the Mayor as chief executive of the City. As with legislators at other levels of 
government, part of the Mayor's expected role is to assist City staff in obtaining funding for local 
government projects, which fulfill a legislative, governmental or charitable purpose and are within 
the scope of City business. 
As an example, at the direction of the City Council, City staff applied for a competitive 
grant from a 501 (c){3) non-profit corporation, for the purpose ofimproving the City's technology 
projects. Because the application process was part of a national competition, it was considered 
advantageous for the City to include a letter of support from the Mayor. Under those circumstances, 
is the Mayor required to report the grant funds on a behested payment report? 
ANALYSIS 
Section 820 l 5(b )(2) provides: 
"A payment made at the behest of a candidate is a contribution to the 
candidate unless the criteria in either subparagraph (A) or (B) are satisfied: 
"(A) Full and adequate consideration is received from the candidate. 
"(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was 
made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective office. The 
following types of payments are presumed to be for purposes unrelated to a 
candidate's candidacy for elective office: 
"(i) A payment made principally for personal purposes, in which 
case it may be considered a gift under the provisions of Section 82028. 
Payments that are otherwise subject to the limits of Section 86203 are 
presumed to be principally for personal purposes. 
"(ii) A payment made by a state, local, or federal governmental 
agency or by a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under 


File No. A-17-206 
Page No. 3 
Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. A payment by a state, 
local, or federal governmental agency that is made principally for 
legislative or governmental purposes is governed exclusively by this 
clause and, therefore, is not subject to the reporting requirement described 
in clause (iii). 
Your question concerns (b)(2)(B)(iii). Payments that fall under this section are subject to 
reporting by the official. 
"A payment not covered by clause (i), made principally for legislative, 
governmental, or charitable purposes, in which case it is neither a gift nor a 
contribution. However, payments of this type that are made at the behest of a 
candidate who is an elected officer shall be reported within 30 days following the 
date on which the payment or payments equal or exceed five thousand dollars 
($5,000) in the aggregate from the same source in the same calendar year in 
which they are made. The report shall be filed by the elected officer with the 
elected officer's agency and shall be a public record subject to inspection and 
copying pursuant to Section 81008. The report shall contain the following 
information: name of payor, address of payor, amount of the payment, date or 
dates the payment or payments were made, the name and address of the payee, a 
brief description of the goods or services provided or purchased, if any, and a 
description of the specific purpose or event for which the payment or payments 
were made. Once the five-thousand-dollar ($5,000) aggregate threshold from a 
single source has been reached for a calendar year, all payments for the calendar 
year made by that source shall be disclosed within 30 days after the date the 
threshold was reached or the payment was made, whichever occurs later. Within 
30 days after receipt of the report, state agencies shall forward a copy of these 
reports to the Commission, and local agencies shall forward a copy of these 
reports to the officer with whom elected officers of that agency file their 
campaign statements." 
By the express terms of the statute, payments solicited by the mayor for a governmental 
purpose must be reported if payments from a single source equal or exceed $5,000. There is no 
exception for payments solicited for the benefit of government employers. 
Note that Section 820 l 5(b )(2)(B)(ii) does exempt a payment made fu!. a state, local, or 
federal governmental agency that is made principally for legislative or governmental purposes from 
behested payment reporting.2 This would not apply to payments solicited for a governmental entity 
made from a nonprofit. 
2 The Legislative Digest for Assembly Bill 1544 (2015) which amended subdivision (b)(2)(B)(ii) states that "a 
payment fu! a governmental agency that is made principally for legislative or governmental purposes at the behest of a 
candidate who is an elected officer . . .  would not be subject to the reporting requirement that applies generally to 
payments made for legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes. " 


File No. A-I 7-206 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
General Counsel 
ision

1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in2. No. The exception would not apply under any of the facts you describe.

sections.analysis:
Section 820 l 5(b )(2) provides: 
"A payment made at the behest of a candidate is a contribution to the 
candidate unless the criteria in either subparagraph (A) or (B) are satisfied: 
"(A) Full and adequate consideration is received from the candidate. 
"(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was 
made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective office. The

"(i) A payment made principally for personal purposes, in which 
case it may be considered a gift under the provisions of Section 82028. 
Payments that are otherwise subject to the limits of Section 86203 are 
presumed to be principally for personal purposes. 
"(ii) A payment made by a state, local, or federal governmental 
agency or by a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under  
Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. A payment by a state, 
local, or federal governmental agency that is made principally for 
legislative or governmental purposes is governed exclusively by this 
clause and, therefore, is not subject to the reporting requirement described 
in clause (iii). 
Your question concerns (b)(2)(B)(iii). Payments that fall under this section are subject to 
reporting by the official. 
"A payment not covered by clause (i), made principally for legislative, 
governmental, or charitable purposes, in which case it is neither a gift nor a 
contribution. However, payments of this type that are made at the behest of a 
candidate who is an elected officer shall be reported within 30 days following the

($5,000) in the aggregate from the same source in the same calendar year in 
which they are made. The report shall be filed by the elected officer with the 
elected officer's agency and shall be a public record subject to inspection and 
copying pursuant to Section 81008. The report shall contain the following

dates the payment or payments were made, the name and address of the payee, a

were made. Once the five-thousand-dollar ($5,000) aggregate threshold from a 
single source has been reached for a calendar year, all payments for the calendar

30 days after receipt of the report, state agencies shall forward a copy of these

federal governmental agency that is made principally for legislative or governmental purposes from

made from a nonprofit. 
2 The Legislative Digest for Assembly Bill 1544 (2015) which amended subdivision (b)(2)(B)(ii) states that "a 
payment fu! a governmental agency that is made principally for legislative or governmental purposes at the behest of a 
candidate who is an elected officer . . .  would not be subject to the reporting requirement that applies generally to 
payments made for legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes. " 


File No. A-I 7-206
